options.E: More aggressive treatment of high blood pressure

explanation.correct: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS) is an uncommon neurologic condition that affects neuromuscular junction transmission. Normally, action potentials lead to depolarization in the terminal bouton of the axon. Voltage-gated calcium channels consequently open and allow calcium influx, which triggers the exocytosis of neurotransmitter-filled vesicles into the synaptic cleft. Patients with LEMS possess antibodies to voltage-gated calcium channels on presynaptic lower motoneurons, which decreases calcium influx and the subsequent release of acetylcholine. This patient with a smoking history and proximal muscle weakness, with a known lung nodule, likely has paraneoplastic autoantibody production against voltage-gated calcium channels, causing LEMS in the setting of small cell lung cancer. Patients may also have depressed or absent deep tendon reflexes. The muscle weakness typically improves with exercise as small amounts of acetylcholine accumulate in the synaptic cleft over time, increasing the frequency of postsynaptic action potentials in skeletal muscle cells. This can be seen on nerve conduction studies, as well. Autonomic symptoms such as decreased salivation (and consequent dry mouth) may also occur. The diagnosis may be confirmed with nerve conduction studies and electromyography with exercise testing. In this patient, smoking cessation would have prevented the development of lung cancer and consequent LEMS.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: B, C, D, and E.
Daily aspirin therapy (Choice B) is used in the prophylaxis and treatment of cardiovascular disease, such as coronary artery disease and stroke. It does not play a role in the prevention of lung cancer development.
Discontinuing atenolol therapy (Choice C) or atorvastatin therapy (Choice D) would not have prevented this patient from developing lung cancer and subsequent LEMS. While β-adrenergic blocker therapy can cause fatigue and statin therapy can cause myopathy, neither causes hyporeflexia.
More aggressive treatment of high blood pressure (Choice E) is an incorrect choice, as it would not have prevented this patient from developing lung cancer. Additionally, this patient's blood pressure is well-controlled.
Educational Objective: Patients with Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome possess antibodies to voltage-gated calcium channels on presynaptic lower motoneurons, which decrease calcium influx and acetylcholine release. This syndrome can be caused by paraneoplastic antibodies, particularly in the setting of small cell lung cancer, which is smoking-related. Patients typically present with progressive proximal muscle weakness that improves with exercise or repeated stimulation.

text: A 30-year-old construction worker comes to the physician because of constant midlower back pain for 1 month. He has no leg pain. Straight-leg raising causes discomfort only in the low back. Sensorimotor examination is normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Restriction of heavy lifting until the pain resolves and instruction in proper body mechanics
options.C: Vocational rehabilitation and change in occupation
options.D: Epidural injection of corticosteroids and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs

explanation.correct: Low back pain is a common condition that is self-limited for many individuals. There are many causes of low back pain, and it rarely can be caused by a serious illness that requires medical treatment or surgical intervention. Risk factors for low back pain include strenuous work, heavy lifting, trauma, obesity, and age. Most patients present with a lumbosacral strain caused by heavy lifting or extended periods of sitting. In patients with a history of trauma or osteoporosis, vertebral compression fractures or other vertebral fractures are a consideration. Signs and symptoms that increase the risk for an urgent medical or surgical cause of low back pain include fever, focal midline spinal tenderness, weight loss, pain increased at night, focal neurologic symptoms, and urinary incontinence or urinary retention. In this patient with no sensorimotor abnormalities, the most likely cause of his low back pain is a lumbosacral strain from heavy lifting and construction work. Most patients with lumbosacral pain will improve within 4 weeks with short-term symptomatic therapy, including pain control, restriction of heavy lifting, physical therapy, and instructions on proper body mechanics, allowing for an early return to activity. Prolonged periods of inactivity and bed rest are to be avoided.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: B, C, D, and E.
Bed rest and oral analgesics (Choice B) would not be the most appropriate management. While oral analgesics can help with pain, early movement and physical therapy has been shown to decrease pain and expedite return of function. Bed rest will increase pain and prolong time to recovery.
Vocational rehabilitation and change in occupation (Choice C) would not be appropriate before a trial of physical therapy. The patient is young and will likely recover after a period of restricting heavy lifting and physical therapy to prevent further injuries.
Epidural injection of corticosteroids and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (Choice D) would also be inappropriate prior to a period of physical therapy and restriction of heavy lifting. Typically, physical therapy and rehabilitation are sufficient to treat lumbosacral strain in a majority of patients.
Intervertebral disc excision and fusion (Choice E) would only be appropriate in the setting of a disc herniation causing severe pain and functional limitation despite a trial of physical therapy. In some cases, disc herniation can cause acute onset neurologic deterioration from spinal cord compression or cauda equina syndrome, which requires emergent surgical management. This patient does not demonstrate any signs of neurologic dysfunction.
Educational Objective: The most common cause of low back pain is a lumbosacral strain. Most patients will improve within 4 weeks with short-term symptomatic therapy, including pain control, restriction of heavy lifting, physical therapy, and instruction on proper body mechanics, allowing for an early return to activity.

text: A 32-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of deep aching neck and shoulder pain, progressive weakness of his hands, and poor coordination. Two years ago, he had a whiplash injury that resolved without treatment over a 3-month period. Neurologic examination shows hyporeflexia of the upper extremities. He has atrophy of intrinsic hand muscles bilaterally. There is asymmetric decreased strength of grip and finger abduction and extension. Sensation to pinprick is decreased over the upper torso and shoulders. Which of the following is the most likely cause of these findings?

explanation.correct: Syringomyelia refers to a progressively expanding cystic lesion involving midline spinal cord structures, most commonly in the cervical or thoracic spinal cord. Syringomyelia may be associated with congenital malformations such as Chiari malformation type I, spinal cord infections, autoimmune diseases such as multiple sclerosis, neoplasms, or spinal cord trauma. Syringomyelia typically compresses spinothalamic fibers decussating in the anterior white commissure. Consequently, patients present with deficits in pain and temperature sensation in the bilateral upper extremities and/or torso. (Since the ascending spinothalamic tracts in the lateral spinal cord are unaffected, sensation in the lower body is intact.) Neuropathic pain in the upper extremities may occur. The lesion can expand to compress the bilateral anterior horns (the location of the cell bodies of lower motoneurons). Therefore, patients may present with a lower motoneuron pattern of dysfunction in the bilateral upper extremities (eg, decreased muscle tone, hyporeflexia, early muscle atrophy). The cyst may progressively expand to compress the lateral corticospinal tract in severe cases, leading to an upper motoneuron pattern of dysfunction in the lower body.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, D, and E.
Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (Choice A) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder that affects the anterior horns and the lateral corticospinal tract. Subsequently, patients demonstrate a combination of upper and lower motoneuron findings in an asymmetric distribution. Sensory impairment is atypical, and a pattern of dysfunction that can be localized to a single lesion within the cervical or thoracic spinal cord is more consistent with a syringomyelia.
Anterior spinal cord syndrome (Choice B) results from ischemia of the anterior spinal artery (eg, by thrombosis or trauma), leading to damage to the corticospinal, spinothalamic, and autonomic tracts. The syndrome manifests with loss of pain and temperature sensation and motor function below the level of the lesion, along with autonomic dysfunction. Syringomyelia causes loss of pain and temperature sensation localized to the upper extremities, and autonomic dysfunction is uncommon in syringomyelia because of the lateral location of autonomic cell bodies.
Brown-Sequard syndrome (Choice C), hemisection of the spinal cord, damages the anterior horn along with the corticospinal tract, dorsal column-medial lemniscus tract, and spinothalamic tract. Patients typically present with contralateral impairment in pain and temperature sensation, ipsilateral deficits in other sensory domains, and an ipsilateral upper motoneuron pattern of dysfunction below the level of the lesion. The dorsal column-medial lemniscus (DCML) tract is typically unaffected in syringomyelia.
Posterior cord syndrome (Choice D) results from ischemia of the posterior spinal artery, leading to ipsilateral DCML tract damage. Patients present with ipsilateral sensory loss of proprioception, fine touch, and vibration below the level of the lesion. The DCML tract is typically unaffected in syringomyelia.
Subacute combined degeneration (Choice E), resulting from vitamin B12 (cobalamin) deficiency, occurs as a result of demyelination of the spinocerebellar tracts, lateral corticospinal tracts, and dorsal columns. The DCML tract is typically unaffected in syringomyelia.
Educational Objective: Syringomyelia features a cystic cavity in the central cervical or thoracic spinal cord, which affects midline structures such as the anterior white commissure (decussation of the spinothalamic tracts) and the anterior horns (cell bodies of lower motoneurons). Patients with syringomyelia may present with deficits in pain and temperature sensation and a lower motoneuron pattern of dysfunction in the bilateral upper extremities and/or torso.

text: A previously healthy 13-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department 1 hour after a generalized tonic-clonic seizure that occurred at school. Her teacher noted that the seizure was preceded by jerking of the right arm. She had a similar episode 8 months ago. Examination at that time showed no abnormalities, and no treatment was begun. She was born in Mexico, and she and her parents have made occasional trips to rural Mexico; their last visit was 2 years ago. She was lethargic on arrival to the emergency department, but she is now awake and alert. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F). The neck is supple. Neurologic examination shows no abnormalities. Her leukocyte count is 6400/mm³. A CT scan of the head is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate screening test for family members of this patient?

options.A: Examination of the stool for ova and parasites

explanation.correct: Neurocysticercosis is a central nervous system (CNS) infection caused by the pork tapeworm Taenia solium that occurs most commonly in endemic rural regions of Latin America, Asia, and Africa. Symptoms include headache, seizures, signs of increased intracranial pressure, and, rarely, altered vision. Some patients are asymptomatic, while other patients may present with muscle or subcutaneous nodules. Typical manifestations on CT or MRI include cystic, ring-enhancing, calcified lesions in the brain parenchyma, meninges, ventricles, spinal cord, and/or orbit. A scolex (larval head) may also be demonstrated on imaging. Treatment includes symptomatic management of increased intracranial pressure and seizures, antiparasitic therapy, and/or steroids. As this patient has primarily traveled with her family, they are similarly at an increased risk for neurocysticercosis and should be screened for the infection with examination of the stool for ova and parasites.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: B, C, D, E, F, and G.
Rapid plasma reagin (Choice B) is used to screen for infection with Treponema pallidum, which causes syphilis. Neurosyphilis may present with headache, altered mental status, vision changes, cranial neuropathies, progressive dementia, and possible brain gummas (eg, focal areas of inflammation). Gummas can be seen on neuroimaging as large, enhancing lesions, but they are not typically calcified.
Stool culture for Coccidioides immitis (Choice C), a dimorphic fungus present in the Southwestern United States and Central and South America, could be used to evaluate for coccidioidomycosis. Coccidioidal meningitis presents subacutely or chronically with headache, nausea and emesis, vision changes, and altered mental status. Neuroimaging may show hydrocephalus or vasculitis with associated infarctions, but it rarely demonstrates intracranial, ring-enhancing, or calcified lesions.
Stool culture for Histoplasma capsulatum (Choice D) can be used to screen for histoplasmosis. H. capsulatum, an endemic mycosis, is primarily found in the Midwestern United States. Disseminated infection most commonly occurs in immunosuppressed patients, whereas isolated infection of the lungs is more common in immunocompetent patients. While disseminated histoplasmosis can present with multiple enhancing brain lesions, it would be unlikely in this otherwise healthy young patient.
Stool culture for Toxoplasma gondii (Choice E) can be used to screen for toxoplasmosis. In immunocompetent patients, the infection is generally asymptomatic, although it can present with a flu-like illness. In immunocompromised patients, however, toxoplasmosis can lead to cerebral infection with multiple ring-enhancing brain lesions. This would be unlikely in this otherwise healthy young patient.
Urine culture for cytomegalovirus (CMV) (Choice F) would not be useful in this scenario. While CMV can cause encephalitis in rare circumstances, it more commonly causes mononucleosis with fevers, fatigue, and cervical lymphadenopathy in an immunocompetent host. It does not present with brain lesions on neuroimaging.
CT scan of the head (Choice G) could evaluate for neurocysticercosis in this patient's family members. However, it would unnecessarily expose them to radiation that could be avoided by utilizing the stool examination for ova and parasites to diagnose an infection, making it a less appropriate next step in management.
Educational Objective: Neurocysticercosis is a CNS infection caused by the pork tapeworm Taenia solium that occurs in endemic rural regions of Latin America, Asia, and Africa and may infect immunocompetent patients. Typical manifestations on CT or MRI include cystic, ring-enhancing, calcified lesions in the brain parenchyma, meninges, ventricles, spinal cord, and/or orbit. Screening for the infection can be performed by examining the stool for ova and parasites.

text: A 6-year-old girl is brought to the physician because of a 1-week history of progressive weakness and numbness of the legs. This morning, she had urinary incontinence. One year ago, she underwent resection of a medulloblastoma and placement of a ventriculoperitoneal shunt for residual hydrocephalus; her postoperative course had been uncomplicated. Currently, she is drowsy and irritable. Her temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F), pulse is 82/min, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 126/54 mm Hg. Funduscopic examination shows no abnormalities. Examination of the lower extremities shows weakness and diffuse sensory loss; deep tendon reflexes are decreased. Which of the following is the most likely underlying mechanism for these symptoms?

options.B: Metastatic spread of the cancer hematogenously
options.C: Metastatic spread of the cancer through cerebrospinal fluid pathways
options.D: Radiation necrosis of the brain stem
options.E: Thrombosis of the anterior spinal artery
options.F: Thrombosis of the superior sagittal sinus

explanation.correct: Metastatic spread of cancer through cerebrospinal fluid pathways is the mechanism behind this patient's development of cauda equina syndrome. Cauda equina syndrome occurs as a result of compression of the spinal nerve roots comprising the cauda equina. Often, such compression occurs because of malignancy (as in this case), trauma, disc herniation, spinal stenosis, spinal epidural abscess, or spinal epidural or subdural hematoma. Symptoms include lower extremity numbness, weakness, paralysis, hyporeflexia, perineal anesthesia, fecal incontinence with poor rectal tone, and overflow incontinence marked by an inability to void with associated urinary retention. Emergent MRI of the lumbosacral spine is indicated to investigate the cause of cauda equina syndrome and to determine the necessity of surgical decompression. Initial treatment for patients with epidural spinal cord compression and neurologic deficits include symptomatic treatment, pain control, and glucocorticoid therapy prior to operative therapy. High-dose glucocorticoid therapy is helpful as a bridge to definitive therapy and may transiently improve neurologic function by decreasing edema at the site of cord compression.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, D, E, and F.
Local recurrence of the medulloblastoma (Choice A) would cause neurologic deficits specific to the region of the brain being affected by the tumor. While, depending on the location, this could cause lower extremity weakness, it would cause hyperreflexia rather than hyporeflexia because of the involvement of the upper motoneuron. This patient's lower extremity hyporeflexia indicates a peripheral cause affecting the lower motoneuron.
Metastatic spread of the cancer hematogenously (Choice B) would be more likely to cause foci of malignancy to develop in other organs including the bones, liver, or lungs. This is an uncommon complication of medulloblastoma since extraneural metastases are rare with this particular tumor.
Radiation necrosis of the brain stem (Choice D) would cause symptoms of midbrain, pons, and medulla infarcts. The cranial nerve nuclei along with ascending and descending spinal tracts would be affected. Symptoms would include impaired cranial nerve function and decreased sensorimotor function of the ipsilateral face and contralateral body. Lower extremity weakness would be accompanied by hyperreflexia, not hyporeflexia, because of the involvement of the upper motoneurons.
Thrombosis of the anterior spinal artery (Choice E) causes dysfunction of the anterior tracts within the spinal cord including the corticospinal tract (loss of motor function), spinothalamic tract (loss of pain and temperature sensation), and anterior horns.
Thrombosis of the superior sagittal sinus (Choice F) is rare because of the large size of this sinus. However, when it does occur, thrombosis in this area results in severe headaches followed by the potential for altered mental status, bilateral neurologic deficits, and seizures. This patient's findings are more consistent with cauda equina syndrome secondary to metastatic spread of cancer.
Educational Objective: Cauda equina syndrome occurs as a result of compression of the spinal nerve roots comprising the cauda equina. It may occur secondary to metastatic spread of cancer through cerebrospinal fluid pathways. Symptoms include lower extremity numbness, weakness, paralysis, hyporeflexia, perineal anesthesia, fecal incontinence with poor rectal tone, and overflow incontinence.

text: A 45-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department 30 minutes after a generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting 1 minute. She had a headache 6 hours ago and nausea and vomiting 2 hours ago. She has no history of seizures and has had no medical problems other than hypertension treated with enalapril and hydrochlorothiazide. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 100/min and regular, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 230/140 mm Hg. She appears drowsy but is responsive to commands. Examination shows no abnormalities other than the funduscopic findings shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy?

explanation.correct: This patient's presenting signs and symptoms of hypertension, tachycardia, altered mental status, tonic-clonic seizures, and optic disc edema are consistent with a diagnosis of hypertensive encephalopathy. Hypertensive encephalopathy may result from acute and severe increases in blood pressure and may manifest with headache, vomiting, agitation, altered mental status, and seizures. It may progress to obtundation and coma. Visual symptoms may result from cortical hypertension or from hypertensive retinopathy. Retinal findings may include optic disc edema and nerve fiber layer infarction or hemorrhage. Treatment includes carefully lowering the blood pressure while avoiding excessively rapid drops in blood pressure, which can cause cerebral or cardiac ischemia. In general, a decrease in the mean arterial pressure of 25% within the first hour is appropriate, with gradual correction over the next day or more following. Sodium nitroprusside is a potent vasodilator and is a preferred agent because of its very short half-life, allowing it to be titrated via intravenous infusion.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, D, and E.
Clonidine (Choice A) is an oral or intravenous antihypertensive agent that stimulates α2-adrenergic receptors. It is commonly used as an adjunct oral agent for the management of hypertensive emergency but is less appropriate as initial pharmacotherapy.
Furosemide (Choice B) is an oral or intravenous loop diuretic. Diuretics are not typically used in the treatment of hypertensive emergency because of their comparatively long half-life and duration of action.
Lisinopril (Choice C) is an oral angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor. Oral agents are not preferred in the initial management of hypertensive emergency.
Propranolol (Choice E) is an oral or intravenous nonselective β-adrenergic blocker. It is not typically used as a primary agent in the treatment of hypertensive emergency because of its comparatively long half-life and duration of action.
Educational Objective: Hypertensive encephalopathy may result from acute and severe increases in blood pressure, and may manifest with headache, vomiting, agitation, altered mental status, and seizures. It may progress to obtundation and coma. Retinal findings may include optic disc edema and nerve fiber layer infarction or hemorrhage. Sodium nitroprusside is a potent vasodilator and is a preferred agent because of its very short half-life, allowing it to be titrated via intravenous infusion.

text:
A 42-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 4-day history of progressive weakness and numbness in her legs and blurred vision and discomfort in both eyes. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Visual acuity is 20/80 bilaterally. Examination shows bilateral optic disc edema. Muscle strength is 5/5 in the upper extremities and 4/5 in the lower extremities. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ in the upper extremities and 4+ in the lower extremities. Babinski sign is present bilaterally. A lumbar puncture is performed. Analysis of the cerebrospinal fluid shows:
Glucose 80 mg/dl
Total protein 105 mg/dl
WBC 50/mm³
RBC 5/mm³
Oligoclonal bands 0 bands
An MRI of the brain shows multiple periventricular white matter lesions. An MRI of the thoracic spine shows an abnormal T2 signal and mild enhancement within the thoracic cord spanning T3 to T6. Which of the following abnormalities is most likely to be noted on further testing in this patient?

options.C: Aquaporin-4 autoantibodies in the cerebrospinal fluid

explanation.correct: Aquaporin-4 autoantibodies in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) are diagnostic of neuromyelitis optica (NMO), a disease characterized by intense inflammation and demyelination of both the optic nerves and various spinal cord segments, with involvement of both grey and white matter. Common manifestations include episodes of optic neuritis, which manifests as vision loss and pain, and transverse myelitis, which can affect any level of the spinal cord. Other unique findings include episodes of hiccups with associated nausea and vomiting. The disease course is often described as relapsing and remitting, with some patients experiencing few symptoms between episodes. Treatment is with high-dose intravenous steroids and, occasionally, plasma exchange for acute attacks. Prophylaxis with eculizumab or other biologic agents is warranted at the time of diagnosis.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, D, and E.
Abnormal echocardiography (Choice A) is seen in patients with strokes secondary to septic emboli from endocarditis, but this patient does not have any evidence of endocarditis, and strokes typically present with unilateral symptoms as opposed to bilateral symptoms, which is more consistent with a spinal cord syndrome.
Antinuclear antibodies (ANA) in the serum (Choice B) are a feature of many rheumatologic diseases, including systemic lupus erythematosus. There is also a portion of the population that has positive ANA titers and no identifiable disease; thus, this is a nonspecific finding.
Decreased peripheral nerve conduction velocities (Choice D) are characteristic of peripheral neuropathy, which usually manifests with sensory changes or weakness of the extremities in a stocking-glove distribution. NMO is a disease of the central nervous system (CNS), and this patient's findings of positive Babinski tests and hyperreflexia are consistent with a central pathology.
Decreased serum copper concentration (Choice E) is not a feature of NMO. Increased copper concentration is seen in Wilson disease, which can present with psychosis, Kayser-Fleischer rings, and cirrhosis.
Educational Objective: NMO is a disease of the CNS with a characteristically relapsing and remitting course that presents with episodes of optic neuritis, transverse myelitis, and intractable hiccups. The presence of aquaporin-4 autoantibodies in the CSF is diagnostic of this disorder.
